chapters.title: No one will know what you want unless you say it
chapters.text:
You might often find yourself surrounded by people in different professions using their work slang at a party. Naturally, you remain silent because some terms or phrases make no sense. However, Lowndes insists that you don't have to keep quiet in situations like this.

Expand your mind first
Each month, dive into something completely new. Attend a wine tasting, volunteer at a food bank, take a coding workshop, or join a book club. You're not becoming an expert; you're collecting conversation currency. When you meet a sommelier at a networking event, you can genuinely say, "I just attended my first wine tasting last month. I was amazed how much the glass shape affected the taste." Instant connection through shared experience.

Sound like you know about their hobby or job
Gobbledygook is the terminology of a profession that will sound like gibberish to other people. But it’s important to learn it because it makes you sound like an insider. Quick learning strategy: Ask an industry insider: "What are the three biggest challenges in your field right now?" This reveals both terminology and hot-button issues.

Keep up with the updates
Before meeting marketing professionals, learn that iOS privacy updates are crushing Facebook ad performance. Before talking to teachers, know that student mental health post-pandemic is a major concern. Drop these topics naturally: "How is your company adapting to the new iOS privacy changes?" shows you understand their world.

Approach professionals from a different angle; find out which burning topics are likely to push their buttons.

Secretly learn about their lives
Is the person you want to meet keen on sports? Or are you going to a gathering of Zen Buddhists, accountants, or anyone similar? Subscribe to a couple of magazines to get insider information from articles or headlines.

When you’re from different countries
Finally, before going to a foreign country, look up the dos and don'ts of that culture. Make sure that your compliments, gifts, or gestures are appropriate.

chapters.title: Fascinate everyone without saying a word
chapters.text:
First impressions are incredibly powerful. They’re holograms that stay in the memory forever, so it’s crucial to get it right at the start. Let’s uncover some tricks.

The exact moment that two humans lay eyes on each other has awesome Potency. ~ Leil Lowndes

To start with:

Make your smile magically different
Here's what most people do wrong: they flash an instant customer-service smile the moment they see someone. Instead, try this. When you meet your colleague in the elevator, look at their face for a second. You notice they seem tired from their morning presentation. Then let a warm, understanding smile spread across your face as you say, "Good morning." They’ll feel genuinely seen, not processed. The rule is simple — adapt your smile to the person in front of you.

Appear intelligent and insightful
When talking to someone, imagine your eyes are gently drawn to theirs like magnets. If you need to look away, do it slowly and reluctantly, as if you're genuinely interested in what they're saying. Try this at your next team meeting when a colleague is speaking and watch how they unconsciously straighten up and speak more confidently.

Make them fall in love with you
At your next dinner party, try this: When someone tells a story, focus completely on them. Watch their reactions, their facial expressions, their body language. They'll feel like the most important person in the room because, to you, they are in that moment.

Look only at the listener, not the speaker. Be genuinely interested in their reactions.

Win people's hearts with a pivot
Picture greeting a delighted toddler who just discovered you exist. That same joy and complete attention is what you give new acquaintances. Remember to turn your entire body toward them, not just your head. For instance, when your networking contact approaches during a conference break, pivot your shoulders, hips, and everything toward them. This full-body welcome signals: "You have my complete attention."

Did you know? In our fast-paced world, we have only 10 seconds to make an indelible first impression.

chapters.title: What follows the “Hi!”
chapters.text:
There are different people you might want to start a conversation with. Some people enjoy small talk; others despise it. The difference between forgettable chatter and memorable conversation isn't the topic — it's your approach, and there's more than one way to make it memorable.

Make a Mood Match
Before you speak, become a detective. Is your coworker rushing between meetings, looking frazzled? Don't bounce over with "Great weather today!" Instead, match their energy: "Looks like a busy morning; how's everything going?" If someone at a party seems contemplative, don't overwhelm them with high energy. Try a calmer approach: "You look like you're taking in the evening. What's your impression so far?"

To practice, read one person's mood each day before deciding how to approach them.

Watch your body language
Your first words don't matter as much as your actions, demeanor, mood, and delivery. When your neighbor asks about your weekend, you could say "Fine" with a shrug, or "It was actually wonderful. I discovered this amazing hiking trail" with genuine enthusiasm. Same information, completely different impact. Your tone, posture, and facial expression carry 80% of the message.

Always wear a Whatzit
Become noticeable within reason. At networking events, conferences, or parties, wear or carry something that invites questions. Maybe it's an interesting watch, a book tucked under your arm, or a unique piece of jewelry. For example, when marketing executive Lisa wears her vintage camera pin to industry events, people consistently ask about it. It’s an instant conversation starter about her passion for photography.

No attention is unwanted if you desire to meet new people. Dress up to fuel curiosity!

Meet who you want with Whoozat
Spotted someone interesting across the room? Don't suffer in silence. Find the host or someone who knows them both and say, "I'd love to meet David; could you introduce me?" Better yet: "Could you tell David I'm the marketing consultant who worked on that campaign he mentioned earlier?" This gives David context and a conversation starter when you're introduced.

Eavesdrop In
Here's how to join that intimidating cluster of people at the office holiday party. Position yourself nearby and listen for 30 seconds. When you hear something you can genuinely contribute to, step closer and say, "Sorry to interrupt, but I couldn't help overhearing you mention the new client project. I actually worked on something similar last year..."

However, if the group seems closed off, simply say "I'll let you continue your conversation" and gracefully move on. No harm done.

chapters.title: Use your knowledge to sound like a VIP
chapters.text:
Studies by the Carnegie Foundation for the Advancement of Teaching and the Carnegie Institute of Technology show that about 85% of a person’s success is due to communication skills. Let’s explore more tips and tricks that will help you.

How to find out what a group is doing
Avoid being bold and asking, "So, what do you do?" Try to be oblique and say: "How do you spend most of your time?". The first is a direct statement that you’re an outsider to the group, whereas the second implies that you already know the basics and now want the specifics.

Have a good response to "What do you do?" with a Nutshell Resume
Smart executives customize their resumes for each job, but you can customize your introduction to each person. To a fellow marketer: "I help companies tell their stories better. I actually got into marketing because I love figuring out what makes people tick." To a tech entrepreneur: "I develop data-driven marketing strategies that typically increase client conversion rates by 30-40%." Same job, different angle based on what resonates with your audience.

Whenever asked, tell the other person about yourself in a unique way.

Sound more brilliant than you are
Replace conversation killers with conversation enhancers. Instead of "That's a good idea," try "That's a compelling approach" or "That's innovative thinking." Replace "pretty cool" with "fascinating" or "remarkable." The goal isn't to sound pretentious; it's to match the energy of your thoughts with more precise language.

chapters.title: Get insider information to fit into the crowd
chapters.text:
You might often find yourself surrounded by people in different professions using their work slang at a party. Naturally, you remain silent because some terms or phrases make no sense. However, Lowndes insists that you don't have to keep quiet in situations like this.

Each month, dive into something completely new. Attend a wine tasting, volunteer at a food bank, take a coding workshop, or join a book club. You're not becoming an expert; you're collecting conversation currency. When you meet a sommelier at a networking event, you can genuinely say, "I just attended my first wine tasting last month. When you’re from different countries
Finally, before going to a foreign country, look up the dos and don'ts of that culture. Make sure that your compliments, gifts, or gestures are appropriate.

chapters.title: How to signal your likeness
chapters.text:
Unlike birds or animals that stick only to their kind, humans love to mingle. We choose others based on how comfortable we are around them and whether they like us back. So, how can you do it successfully?

We take the most liking to the people who share our values.

Make them feel you are of the same "class"
Observe how others move and gesture respectfully. Do they look classy or unsure? Do they move fast or slow? As you do, pretend you're watching a dance class instructor explain the moves. Watch their movement style, and then try to tune in.

But always remember to balance. Ask yourself, "Am I adjusting to show respect for their style, or am I being inauthentic?" The first builds bridges, and the second… feels manipulative.

Make them feel that you are like "family"
Use their words, not yours. Listen carefully to their word choice; what adjectives, nouns, and verbs do they use? Pick some of them and incorporate them into your speech. Hearing these words, they will feel like you know and understand them; that you share the same attitudes and values.

So, if someone describes their project as "challenging," use "challenging" instead of "difficult" when discussing it later. If they say something was "fantastic," respond with "that does sound fantastic" rather than "great." They'll subconsciously feel more understood and connected.

Learn to empathize
To say that you agree, do you use "yeah," "mmm," or the like? While that shows you agree, it doesn't make your conversation partner want to share more with you. Instead of "umming," say phrases like "I see what you mean" or "That's a wonderful thing to say." Such words encourage them to continue the conversation.

chapters.title: How to praise but not flatter
chapters.text:
Let’s talk about praise. Studies have shown that compliments from a new person have a more powerful impact than from someone you already know. But how can you compliment correctly without flattering and going too far?

You can kill your entire relationship with one mishandled compliment, so choose wisely.

Learn to compliment anyone
Imagine hearing someone compliment you to someone else "behind your back." Of course, you’ll feel thrilled and flattered. A good starting point is to talk to your friends about people you like or admire.

Let’s imagine you work with Sarah and Jessica, and they’re friends. When you tell Jessica that Sarah's presentation was brilliant and that she has such a gift for making complex ideas clear, she will probably mention it to Sarah. It carries ten times the weight of you saying it directly.

Be a carrier of good feelings
Be a metaphorical pigeon and carry good news and praise to others. When you notice someone talking about another person in a good light, compliment them. You may not receive a medal for it, but everyone loves the pigeon who bears kind thoughts and good news.

For example, when your boss mentions how impressed she was with Tom's client presentation, make sure Tom hears about it: "Lisa told me she was really impressed with how you handled the Johnson account — said you made it look effortless."

Sing your praises as if they were a slip of the tongue
In a conversation, sprinkle positive comments about the person you are talking with. Yet, instead of stopping the conversation to say, "You're so smart," let it emerge organically: "When you explain the budget breakdown like that, it suddenly makes perfect sense — you have a gift for making numbers tell a story." It feels authentic rather than forced, and they feel genuinely seen for their abilities.

They'll never forget you with a "killer compliment"
It’s time to make yourself memorable. While you’re talking, notice a unique thing you like about them. At the end of the conversation, look them straight in the eye, say their name, and finish with the killer compliment, like "What gorgeous eyes you have," or "You have a wonderful air of honesty about you."

chapters.title: Get directly into their hearts
chapters.text:
Many people know you only by communicating via the phone or messages. Yet, they never get to enjoy being around you in the offline world. So, let them know who you are, even when talking on the phone.

Your personality, mine, and everyone's could be likened to a show, a theatrical performance. You want to make sure yours is a box-office smash, not a flop. ~ Leil Lowndes

Inspire people even over the phone
Whenever you talk on the phone, imagine you’re the star of a radio show. They can’t see you, so you need to voice what would be non-verbal cues. Make sure they hear your smile, nods, or gestures by turning them into sounds. Replace your gestures with talk, then boost the whole act by 30%!

Sound like you’re close by
Address people by their first name to get their attention. Saying their name equals the eye contact you might give if they were in front of you. Sure, in person, it may sound like flirting, but there’s more distance between you on a call, so feel free to use their name once in a while.

People engage in conversation more when you say their name.

Make them happy that they called you
You should always be professional during work calls, but you can still show warmth. When you find out who you’re speaking with, break into a smile that floods your face and shows in your voice. The listener will feel that you’re especially happy to hear them. You can also breathe out gently as you break into a smile. Even for business calls, you can convey: "I was just thinking about our project. Perfect timing." This makes people feel valued rather than just another item on your to-do list.

Sneak past the gatekeeper
If your employees screen your incoming calls, tell them to greet the caller joyfully and ask for their name. When they identify themselves, have your employee repeat their name before putting the caller through to you. That way, if your secretary informs them that you’re unavailable, they won’t take it to heart or feel screened.

Train your assistant to say: "Good morning! This is Jennifer from David's office. May I ask who's calling so I can let him know you're on the line?" If you're unavailable: "David is in a client meeting until 3 PM. Would you like me to have him call you back today, or would scheduling a specific time work better?" It feels helpful, not dismissive.

chapters.title: You don’t have to be a politician to work a party
chapters.text:
Before going to a party, study the list of invited people. Smart politicians and VIPs arrive a little earlier to get their business done with their people of interest. If you want to be among them, get there ahead of time.

Avoid the common party blooper
Experienced politicians and negotiators know that if you want to have a good conversation, you should never eat at a party. You have to choose between eating or mingling and never do both. Professional tip: If you must eat, grab something small during a natural break in conversations, then return to networking mode.

When attending a party, clear your priorities. Zone in on what you want to achieve.

Make an unforgettable entrance
When you arrive at a gathering, make a dramatic stop at the doorway and slowly study the situation, darting your eyes back and forth. Your purpose is not to show off but to survey what is already happening so that you know where and how to fit in. Look for familiar faces or locate the people you’d like to meet.

Meet the people you really want to meet
You can be proactive and the one who chooses. Find the person you like in the room, and don’t wait for others to approach you. Make the first step, and maybe you’ll meet the love of your life, your best friend, or a long-term business partner.

You can also prepare two to three opening questions before events: "How do you know the host?" "What brings you to this event?" "What's your take on [relevant industry topic]?" Having these ready reduces anxiety and makes approaching people feel more natural.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Understanding human nature and common habits makes it easier to develop communication skills and improve relationships. By using the techniques we’ve discussed, you’ll gain the confidence you need to make friends, attract the people you want in your life, and find reliable business partners.

Remember, no one is born with a perfect set of communication techniques. We learn these things as we grow, developing and enhancing them over time. Some people may seem more confident because they know how to approach others. Yet, most of the time, charisma has nothing to do with it.

Try this

• Before jumping into a conversation, assess the other person’s mood.
• Listen to their voice carefully to sense their emotional state of mind. Do they sound worried, angry, or perfectly fine?
• Then, take a look at their facial expressions. Do they support the idea you got from voice sampling?
• Finally, match their mood to their tone of voice before starting to talk.

Read it till the end?
